does: Understands very simple office procedures and terminology (e.g., filing, answering phones).
Knows how to follow straightforward instructions.

Knows how to follow straightforward instructions.
Uses basic office software (word processors, spreadsheets) through menu commands; knowledge is limited to simple commands and templates.
Knows routine clerical procedures such as logging forms, routing mail, or checking documents for completeness.
May perform data entry or file maintenance using a single system.

May perform data entry or file maintenance using a single system.
Applies a standardized body of regulations and procedures to carry out recurring tasks like processing forms, screening files, or explaining routine benefits.
Recognizes multiple, interrelated steps but follows prescribed sequences without needing to adapt procedures.
Uses one or two automated systems to accomplish tasks (e.g., scanning documents).
Understands very simple office procedures and terminology (e.g., filing, answering phones).
Knows how to follow straightforward instructions.
Uses basic office software (word processors, spreadsheets) through menu commands; knowledge is limited to simple commands and templates.
Knows routine clerical procedures such as logging forms, routing mail, or checking documents for completeness.
May perform data entry or file maintenance using a single system.
Applies a standardized body of regulations and procedures to carry out recurring tasks like processing forms, screening files, or explaining routine benefits.
Recognizes multiple, interrelated steps but follows prescribed sequences without needing to adapt procedures.
Uses one or two automated systems to accomplish tasks (e.g., scanning documents).

Possesses practical knowledge of multiple automated systems or software packages (e.g., web editors, photo editing, intranet platforms) to manage information; may maintain intranet pages or combine information from different systems.
Knows organizational policies, structure and administrative procedures; able to coordinate across offices and track status.
Applies well-defined methods to gather information and prepare products such as reports, budgets, or schedules; uses off-the-shelf tools rather than creating software.
Understands very simple office procedures and terminology (e.g., filing, answering phones).
Knows how to follow straightforward instructions.
Uses basic office software (word processors, spreadsheets) through menu commands; knowledge is limited to simple commands and templates.
Knows routine clerical procedures such as logging forms, routing mail, or checking documents for completeness.
May perform data entry or file maintenance using a single system.
Applies a standardized body of regulations and procedures to carry out recurring tasks like processing forms, screening files, or explaining routine benefits.
Recognizes multiple, interrelated steps but follows prescribed sequences without needing to adapt procedures.
Uses one or two automated systems to accomplish tasks (e.g., scanning documents).
Possesses practical knowledge of multiple automated systems or software packages to manage information; may maintain intranet pages or combine information from different systems.
Knows organizational policies, structure and administrative procedures; able to coordinate across offices and track status.
Applies well-defined methods to gather information and prepare products such as reports, budgets, or schedules.

Knowledge of a comprehensive body of technical rules and procedures for an administrative specialty (e.g., HR assistant). Able to research, interpret and apply a wide range of guidance to resolve routine problems and answer questions.
Conducts interviews, analyzes factual information and explains procedural requirements to employees and supervisors.
Provides advice on minor problems such as processing appeals or rating employees using established criteria.
Understands very simple office procedures and terminology.
Knows how to follow straightforward instructions.
Uses basic office software through menu commands.
Knows routine clerical procedures and performs data entry or file maintenance.
Applies a standardized body of regulations and procedures.
Recognizes multiple, interrelated steps but follows prescribed sequences.
Uses one or two automated systems to accomplish tasks.
Possesses practical knowledge of multiple automated systems to manage information; coordinates across offices.
Applies well-defined methods to gather information and prepare products.
Possesses knowledge of a comprehensive body of technical rules and procedures and provides advice on minor problems.
Skilled in applying analytical and evaluative techniques (e.g., literature searches, work measurement, task analysis, productivity charting) to gather and organize factual data.
Understands management and organizational theory, administrative practices, and basic budget and human resources principles.
Conducts interviews and observes work processes to identify procedural problems and recommend improvements.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Thorough knowledge of laws, regulations, policies, and precedents affecting a major mission function; understands program goals and objectives.
Plans, schedules and conducts projects to improve program effectiveness and productivity; gathers, analyzes and interprets diverse data and prepares recommendations.
Adapts analytic techniques and evaluation criteria to study different programs; considers interrelationships among programs and functions.
Skill in effectively communicating with managers and officials to present findings and gain agreement.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Mastery of advanced qualitative and quantitative methods and comprehensive knowledge of administrative laws, policies, and precedent-setting regulations.
Designs and directs broad, complex or highly sensitive studies; evaluates proposed legislation or agency regulations and recommends major program changes.
Develops new measurement methods and evaluation criteria and leads multi-disciplinary teams; negotiates with high-level officials on controversial issues.
Represents the organization in discussions where program or policy developments are formulated.
Supervisor provides very detailed instructions on assignments and methods; tasks involve simple, repetitive steps.
Work is closely controlled and checked during and after completion; employee has little or no latitude.
Supervisor provides very detailed instructions on assignments and methods; tasks involve simple, repetitive steps.
Work is closely controlled and checked during and after completion.
Supervisor gives general instructions covering what is to be done, deadlines and priorities; tasks are recurring and well defined.
Employee uses judgment to complete tasks but refers unfamiliar situations or deviations to supervisor.
Work is reviewed for accuracy, adherence to instructions and completion of objectives.
Supervisor provides very detailed instructions and general instructions for recurring tasks.
Work is closely controlled but employee uses judgment to complete tasks and refers unusual situations to the supervisor.
Supervisor outlines objectives and sets deadlines but relies on employee to plan and execute work; assignments may be varied and involve analysis or problem solving.
Employee chooses methods, resolves most problems independently, and informs supervisor of unusual issues.
Supervisor reviews completed work for consistency with policy and practicality, not step-by-step compliance.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Employee and supervisor jointly develop project plans, discussing scope, methods and resources; once agreed, the employee carries out all phases of assignments.
Supervisor provides administrative guidance (e.g., budget, time frames) but rarely intervenes; employee has latitude to resolve issues.
Finished work is reviewed for compatibility with organizational goals and policies, not for methodology.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Employee receives only administrative direction outlining broad objectives; supervisor trusts the employee to determine approaches, plan projects and set priorities.
Assignments often involve novel or precedent‑setting issues; the employee makes authoritative determinations and exercises full responsibility over analysis and final recommendations.
Supervisor reviews results only for policy implications or potential impact on the program.
Uses detailed and specific guidelines, such as standard operating procedures, manuals, and software instructions.
Steps are clear‑cut and cover almost every situation; employee is expected to follow instructions exactly.
Uses detailed and specific guidelines and follows clear‑cut steps.
Multiple established guidelines (regulations, policy directives, vendor manuals) cover most aspects of the work; employee must select the correct guideline for each case.
Employee may make minor adjustments to fit specific circumstances but generally does not deviate from instructions.
Unusual situations or conflicts are referred to the supervisor or a higher authority.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Standard references and precedent studies are available but often contain gaps or are not directly applicable to the specific problem.
Employee researches regulations, adapts or interprets guidelines and determines their interrelationships to solve problems.
May develop new procedures when existing guidelines do not cover the situation; uses judgment to balance efficiency and compliance.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Guidelines consist of general administrative policies and broad management directives that state desired results but not specific methods.
Employee must research policy intent, adapt and extend guidelines and develop more specific instructions, procedures, or criteria.
Develops new methods or modifies existing procedures to improve program effectiveness.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Guidelines are very general, such as policy statements, legislative histories, and precedent‑setting decisions; often ambiguous or contradictory.
Employee interprets and revises existing policy, formulates new guidance, and drafts policy or regulatory provisions for adoption by top management.
Serves as an expert authority on guidelines, advising other analysts and management on their interpretation and application.
Work consists of simple, clear‑cut and repetitive tasks such as verifying forms, scanning documents or performing data entry.
Actions follow prescribed steps and require little or no choice in what to do or how to do it.
Work consists of simple, clear‑cut and repetitive tasks.
Assignments include related procedural steps; employee must choose the correct sequence and apply established methods.
Decisions involve selecting from among well‑established procedures or options based on customer needs or organizational requirements.
Work may involve searching files, selecting the proper code, or applying standard formulas; there may be some variation in how to proceed.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Assignments involve procedural problems where the employee applies analytic techniques to collect and organize data (e.g., task analysis, workload measurement).
Work requires selecting from a variety of established methods and making adjustments to fit specific situations; products include narrative reports and tables.
Problems are typically factual rather than substantive; decisions depend on comparing data to standards or precedents.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Assignments involve substantive or programmatic problems where data are incomplete or conflicting.
Employee applies qualitative and quantitative analytical techniques, determines the nature of problems, and develops recommendations to improve program effectiveness.
Requires originality in refining existing methods and interpreting regulations to suit local conditions.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Projects address complex, interrelated program or administrative issues that span functions or organizations.
Employee develops long‑range plans, new measurement criteria, and procedures to evaluate program effectiveness or efficiency.
Assignments may involve evaluating subjective information and conflicting objectives; recommendations influence major programs.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Assignments involve extremely broad and undefined program issues; studies often affect multiple agencies, national policies or public programs.
Employee leads teams of subject‑matter experts to develop new concepts, translate legislative or regulatory intent into programs, and evaluate the impact of proposed policies.
Requires creating new ways to measure and implement programs and developing long‑range strategic plans that significantly redirect Federal efforts.
Work consists of repetitive or strictly defined tasks such as maintaining records, entering data or distributing information.
Products primarily facilitate the work of colleagues within the immediate office and have little impact beyond the unit.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Assignments involve collecting and disseminating information or administering processes (e.g., verifying forms, scheduling tests) that affect the timeliness and accuracy of administrative operations.
Work products contribute to the convenience of operations but do not influence policies or change program effectiveness.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Projects improve efficiency and productivity of administrative support; employee identifies problems, collects data and provides recommendations.
Reports influence managers' decisions and may change how resources or services are delivered but do not include evaluation of program effectiveness or the overall mission.
Scope typically is limited to internal administrative functions or portions of broader studies.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Work assesses productivity and effectiveness of program operations, not just administrative support.
Develops criteria to measure achievement of program goals and recommends improvements that may affect multiple organizational levels or cooperating agencies.
Recommendations are adopted by managers and have a direct and lasting impact on mission delivery.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Assignments involve analysis and resolution of major administrative aspects of mission‑oriented programs.
Work leads to development of new program regulations, policies, or evaluation criteria and may affect large segments of society or multiple agencies.
Recommendations cause significant changes in program operations and resource allocations across a bureau or region.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Work encompasses very broad studies and recommendations that realign responsibilities across programs or agencies, often influencing national policy.
Assignments address issues of such scope that they change legislation, program structure, or federal approaches; may involve inter‑agency coordination and public impact.
Outcomes may significantly redirect Federal resources and programs.
Contacts are within the immediate office or work group (e.g., coworkers, supervisor).
Purpose is to exchange routine information or receive assignments.
Contacts are within the immediate office or work group.
Purpose is to exchange routine information or receive assignments.
Contacts are with employees of the same agency or recurring business contacts outside the agency, such as vendors or local contractors.
May also involve contacts with the public to obtain or provide factual information.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Contacts include program officials several managerial levels away, consultants, contractors, or representatives of other agencies or community organizations.
Contacts may occur in formal settings such as committees or working groups.
The people contacted typically have decision‑making authority or specialized knowledge needed for the project.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Contacts are with top‑level agency officials, major program executives, key staff of legislative or judicial bodies, or corporate presidents.
The contacts typically involve those who have the authority to set policy or commit resources.
Contacts are to obtain, clarify, or provide factual information, such as exchanging status updates, explaining procedures, or answering routine questions.
There is no attempt to influence the other party's opinions or decisions.
Contacts are to obtain, clarify or provide factual information.
Provides advice, guidance, or limited training, such as explaining administrative procedures, HR policies, or program processes to employees or supervisors.
May resolve routine problems or answer technical questions; the contacts may require using tact and diplomacy to explain reasons for policies.
Contacts are to obtain, clarify or provide factual information and to offer advice or limited training.
Contacts are to influence, persuade, or motivate managers, officials, or contractors to adopt recommendations, change methods, or accept findings.
Requires skill in negotiation and presentation of arguments; the employee may propose solutions and advocate for their adoption.
Contacts are to obtain, clarify or provide factual information, offer advice, and persuade or influence others.

Contacts are to justify, defend, or settle matters of considerable significance such as major program proposals, contract disputes, or policy changes with officials who have decision‑making authority.
Requires negotiating with parties having opposing viewpoints and often involves compromise to reach agreement.
Work is primarily sedentary; employee sits at a desk or computer most of the time.
May require occasional walking, standing, or carrying light items such as folders or pamphlets.
Requires no special physical effort.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Work requires long periods of standing, bending, or stooping to observe work operations or perform tasks such as filing and mail handling.
May involve moderate physical exertion, including lifting and carrying packages, mail trays, or boxes.
Work is performed in a typical office setting that is adequately lighted, heated, and ventilated.
Involves everyday safety risks requiring normal precautions; no special safety equipment is needed.
<REF_PRIOR_LEVEL_DUTIES>
Duties include regular visits to manufacturing, industrial or mail‑processing areas where there may be moving machinery, loud noise or other hazards.
Employee uses protective clothing or safety equipment when needed and exercises extra safety precautions.
